NORM: Normal sinus rhythm with regular R-R intervals and a physiologic rate.
Upright P wave preceding every QRS with a normal PR interval.
Narrow QRS complexes with normal R-wave progression across precordial leads.
No pathological Q waves and no significant ST-segment deviation.
Normal T-wave orientation and a normal frontal-plane QRS axis.
MI: Pathological Q waves or QS complexes in contiguous leads.
Localized ST-segment elevation indicating acute injury.
Poor R-wave progression with loss of anterior forces.
T-wave inversion in the territory of the affected coronary artery.
Reciprocal ST-segment changes in opposite leads.
STTC: ST-segment depression without a clear infarction pattern.
Flattened or inverted T waves suggesting repolarization abnormality.
Biphasic T waves and nonspecific ST-T changes.
Ischemic-like repolarization changes across multiple leads.
Abnormal T-wave axis with lead-specific ST-T deviation.
CD: Prolonged QRS duration consistent with a conduction delay.
Right bundle branch block morphology with rSR' in V1.
Left bundle branch block morphology with broad notched R waves.
Prolonged PR interval indicating atrioventricular conduction delay.
Fascicular block with associated frontal-plane axis deviation.
HYP: Increased QRS voltage meeting left ventricular hypertrophy criteria.
Tall R waves in lateral leads with deep S waves in the right precordium.
Repolarization strain pattern with ST-T changes opposite the QRS.
Left atrial abnormality with a broad notched P wave.
Right ventricular hypertrophy pattern with right axis deviation.
